Name: training_free_latency_aware_inference
Title: PAC-Bayes-Driven Training-Free Latency-Aware Inference Optimization
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds, we can develop a training-free, latency-aware inference optimization method that minimizes hidden-state perturbation and enforces strict wall-clock budgets.
Related Work: Existing work on mixed-precision quantization (e.g., SNIP, AMP-ViT) and training-free proxies (e.g., EMQ) primarily focuses on training-time optimizations or post-training quantization. Our proposal differs by targeting real-time inference optimization with strict latency constraints, leveraging PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds to ensure minimal hidden-state perturbation and enforce strict wall-clock budgets.
Abstract: Real-time inference optimization with strict latency constraints remains a significant challenge in machine learning. Existing methods primarily focus on training-time optimizations or post-training quantization, often neglecting real-time constraints. This paper introduces a novel training-free, latency-aware inference optimization method leveraging PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds. Our approach minimizes hidden-state perturbation and enforces strict wall-clock budgets, ensuring efficient and effective real-time inference. We propose a Jacobian-based layer-skipping framework that directly targets ML inference theory, minimizing hidden-state perturbation and enforcing strict wall-clock budgets. Our experiments demonstrate significant improvements in throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers, with explicit perturbation-cost and memory-bandwidth accounting. This work bridges foundational ML theory with system-scale inference, offering a robust solution for latency-critical applications.

Experiments.Description: Evaluate the proposed method on standard benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet, CIFAR-10) using various model architectures (e.g., ResNet, MobileNet).
Compare the proposed method with state-of-the-art mixed-precision quantization methods (e.g., SNIP, AMP-ViT) and training-free proxies (e.g., EMQ).
Conduct ablation studies to analyze the impact of different components of the proposed method (e.g., Jacobian-based layer-skipping, spectral stability bounds).

Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed method may not generalize well to all model architectures and datasets.
Strict latency constraints may limit the applicability of the method in certain scenarios.
The computational overhead of the Jacobian-based layer-skipping framework may impact overall performance.
